synopsis: Pirate Prentice (P06) wakes from an evacuation dream in a Chelsea maisonette and climbs to his rooftop banana garden, where a V-2 vapour trail hangs over the North Sea at dawn. He harvests bananas while waiting for the rocket to arrive. The novel opens not with characters but with conditions: dread, physics, absurdist survival beneath the Blitz.

Pirate Prentice (P06) prepares a lavish banana breakfast for his household while Osbie Feel sings bawdy songs on the rooftop. A phone call dispatches him to retrieve a message from inside the fallen rocket, revealing his peculiar talent: he can enter and manage the fantasies of others, serving British intelligence as a surrogate dreamer. A giant Adenoid devouring London blooms from one such borrowed fantasy, imperial paranoia rendered as music-hall burlesque. Pirate's capacity for absorbing other minds foreshadows the novel's question of who owns the inner life when institutions can requisition imagination itself.

Teddy Bloat slips into the ACHTUNG office to photograph a map on Slothrop's (P01) wall, a map of London covered in coloured stars marking his sexual conquests. The stars form a pattern whose significance nobody yet grasps, but someone in Pointsman's (P05) orbit is already watching. The episode establishes the novel's founding surveillance: Slothrop's private life has become an object of intelligence, his body a text to be read by men he has never met.

Slothrop (P01), an American lieutenant adrift in wartime London, marks his sexual conquests on a map while trading drinks with his friend Tantivy Mucker-Maffick at the Snipe and Shaft. Beneath the bravado lies terror: V-2 rockets arrive before their own sound, and a man can be dead before he hears the thing that killed him. The episode traces the Slothrop family's long decline through Puritan New England, from fur traders to trust-fund ghosts, and arrives at the founding anomaly: in September 1944, a rocket bracketed Slothrop on Bond Street, and he got an erection. Pointsman's (P05) programme will seize on this coincidence, but the novel has already named its epistemology: paranoia as Puritan inheritance, the conviction that everything is connected and someone is always watching.

At a séance in Snoxall's, the medium Carroll Eventyr channels the spirit Peter Sachsa, who speaks the name 'Dominus Blicero (P02)' for the first time. In the next room, Pirate Prentice (P06) trades microfilm with Roger Mexico (P06), remembering his doomed affair with Scorpia Mossmoon, an executive's wife whose betrayal left him hollowed. Jessica Swanlake throws a dart into dead centre. The episode introduces the occult countercurrent running beneath Pointsman's (P05) scientific rationalism: the dead speak, the living listen, and neither party controls the exchange.

Roger Mexico (P06) drives Jessica through blacked-out streets to the White Visitation, where Pointsman (P05) needs dogs for his Pavlovian experiments. The Roger-Jessica love affair unfolds in full: how they met on the road to Battle when a rocket fell, their illegal house under the barrage balloons, the fierce private warmth against the public cold. The War is their context and their permission; they are trying to secede from it, knowing the armistice will return them to the world of proper boyfriends and institutional claims.

Slapstick in the ruins: Roger (P06), Jessica, and Pointsman (P05) chase a stray dog through bombed-out London in the rain, Pointsman hobbling with a toilet bowl stuck on his foot. The dog escapes every attempt at capture, and the comedy barely conceals the violence underneath. These are streets where people died, and the Pavlovian researcher chasing a dog through rubble is already hunting for a human subject.

Pointsman (P05) and the neurologist Spectro sit in the hospital at night, listening to rocket-blast survivors relive their trauma in the abreaction ward. They discuss Slothrop's (P01) case: the V-2 reverses stimulus and response, explosion arriving before sound, and Slothrop seems to respond sexually before the rockets fall. Pointsman wants a human experimental subject, and his clinical hunger barely masks the desire for total control over another man's reflexes.

Jessica (P06) wakes from a nightmare and smokes in the secret house she shares with Roger. He explains his Poisson distribution: every square on the map is equally likely, and bombs have no memory. Their fragile domesticity, with its flowers and chickens and table with one weak leg, exists in defiance of Pointsman's (P05) deterministic universe. A rocket hits close, slamming the window inward; death stands in the pantry doorway, patient as iron.

Slothrop (P01) under sodium amytal, dosed by Pointsman's (P05) programme: a hallucinatory plunge through a Roxbury jazz club, down a toilet into an underworld of Harvard memories, past Jack Kennedy and Malcolm X as shoeshine boy, through a surreal American West with Crutchfield the Westwardman. The drugged consciousness is structured as variations on the phrase 'You never did the Kenosha Kid', each iteration dissolving the boundary between subject and sentence. A phantasmagoric American descent, comic and obscene, racially charged and linguistically unhinged, with the experimenters listening from the other side of the needle.

Pirate Prentice (P06) receives a sexually charged drawing, ejaculates onto an accompanying blank page to reveal a message written in Kryptosam, an invisible ink developed by Laszlo Jamf (P04) for IG Farben and activated by semen. Jamf's chemistry reaches into the most private act imaginable; his inventions do not merely support the war machine but colonise the body itself. Pirate's private fantasies are known and exploited by intelligence services operating within Pointsman's (P05) institutional world. A man reduced to a decoding instrument, desire and industrial chemistry fused into a single act of compliance.

The White Visitation introduced in full: a Gothic folly on the Sussex cliffs requisitioned for PISCES, housing Brigadier Pudding's bewildered command, Pointsman's (P05) scheming, the Pavlovian dog experiments, and Operation Black Wing, a propaganda scheme involving fictional black rocket troops. Dog Vanya salivates on cue while a dozen wartime agencies jostle for postwar survival. Slothrop's (P01) name circulates in the corridors as a case file, a problem to be solved; the architecture of institutional paranoia takes shape around him.

The central revelation: Laszlo Jamf (P04), a German chemist visiting Harvard around 1920, conditioned infant Slothrop (P01) to respond to a mysterious stimulus. The stars on Slothrop's sexual map match Roger Mexico's (P06) V-2 strike map, each star preceding its corresponding rocket by four and a half days. Pointsman (P05) and Mexico walk a frozen beach debating cause and effect; Pointsman wants to break Slothrop open, while Mexico fears for the man. Jamf's experiment binds chemistry, sexuality, and the rocket into a single knot that the novel will spend three hundred pages trying to untie.

The longest episode in Part 1. A camera films Katje Borgesius (P02) moving through Pirate's flat while Osbie Feel prepares hallucinogenic mushrooms in the kitchen. From this present the narrative plunges into Katje's past: sadomasochistic captivity with Captain Blicero at a V-2 launch site in the Netherlands, where she and the boy Gottfried enact a Hansel-and-Gretel script of sacrifice and transcendence. Blicero's history reaches back to South-West Africa and a Herero lover named Enzian (P03). Katje's ancestor Frans van der Groov exterminated the dodo on Mauritius; at the White Visitation, film of Katje is screened daily for Grigori the octopus as a conditioning experiment. Colonial genocide, sexual bondage, rocket technology, and cinematic manipulation braided into one knot.

Slothrop (P01) returns to the London streets sensing he is being followed. His desk has been disturbed, girls cancel appointments, anomalies appear in every mirror and cinema queue. In the East End, old Mrs. Quoad subjects him to a tragicomic ordeal of revolting English sweets, Marmalade Surprises and Meggezones that freeze his skull; afterwards, lying with Darlene, a rocket hits nearby and his erection springs up with the blast. Someone is watching through the window, and the body that was conditioned in infancy performs on cue.

Roger (P06) and Jessica's love in fragments: their first touch, their invented 'cute meet' scenarios, her body in red light, Roger's agony over Jeremy, her absent proper boyfriend. Near her anti-aircraft battery on an evening before Christmas, they stop at a country church for evensong. The episode expands into a vast meditation on Advent: toothpaste tubes recycled for munitions, a black Jamaican countertenor singing above the congregation, the war-town's desolation held briefly at bay by the music. Can anything be redeemed tonight, and if so, for how long?

Pointsman (P05) sleeps through a thousand bombers but wakes to Gwenhidwy's soft knock: St. Veronica's has been hit by a rocket, and Spectro is dead. The five co-owners of Pavlov's Book are being killed off one by one in escalating sophistication of weapon, and Pointsman fears a curse. His inner life unfurls in the small hours: loneliness, erotic fantasies of the Nobel Prize, a dream of confronting a Minotaur. Slothrop (P01) is his last chance, the human subject who might vindicate everything before the rockets and the budgets run out.

Carroll Eventyr's mediumship explored from inside: his 'splendid weakness' discovered by Nora Dodson-Truck on the Embankment years ago. The White Visitation's psychic freaks are catalogued in full: Ronald Cherrycoke, Gavin Trefoil who changes skin colour, Margaret Quartertone who records voices at a distance, all of them Pointsman's reluctant instruments. On the other side of death, Peter Sachsa recalls 1920s Berlin: séances, drugs, women in jade, and a Herero aide brought by Lieutenant Weissmann from South-West Africa. The episode maps the paranormal underside of the institution, where the irrational is put to bureaucratic use.

Weimar Berlin, early 1930s: Leni Pökler has left her husband Franz (P04), taking their daughter Ilse to a cold squat among leftist students. The episode braids Leni's political radicalism against Franz's passive rocket-mysticism, his dreams of escape velocity set against her demand for engagement with the present. Franz stumbles upon a rocket static-test at Reinickendorf, a tiny silver egg roaring in the dark, and meets Kurt Mondaugen. A séance to contact the assassinated Walter Rathenau delivers a prophetic lecture on coal-tar chemistry and the death-worship hidden inside industrial cartelisation.

Christmas Day at the White Visitation. Pointsman (P05) is elated: Slothrop (P01) is on his way to the Riviera, delivered into the experiment at last. At the PISCES Christmas party, Maudie Chilkes pulls him into a closet; afterwards, the enormous Welsh doctor Thomas Gwenhidwy walks with Pointsman through falling snow and delivers his 'City Paranoiac' theory: London itself has arranged for the East End poor to absorb the rocket strikes while Whitehall is spared. They drink Vat 69 and toast 'the babies', and cockroaches process toward the larder in a long, purposeful column.

Boxing Day at Jessica's (P06) sister's house. The children have been to a pantomime of Hansel and Gretel, and when a rocket fell near the theatre the actress playing Gretel sang a defiant music-hall song to quiet the crying babies. The fairy-tale that will consume Pökler (P04) and Ilse in its industrial version is here rendered as tinsel and greasepaint, harmless and brave. In the kitchen Roger holds Jessica's cold hands against his chest, knowing Slothrop (P01) has already been sent south and the machinery is accelerating; she may leave him for Jeremy and the rationalised power-ritual of the coming peace. Part 1 ends with his silent plea: Don't leave me.

Slothrop (P01) arrives at the Casino Hermann Goering on the Riviera, giddy with relief at leaving the Blitz behind. On the beach with Tantivy and three French dancers, an enormous octopus seizes a young blonde woman from the shallows; Slothrop clubs at it with a wine bottle and rescues Katje Borgesius (P02), who gazes at him with unsettling recognition. Paranoia blooms instantly: the crab was too conveniently at hand, the octopus too deliberate, and the reader already knows Katje carries Blicero's history in her body. Pointsman's (P05) programme has delivered its subject to the controlled environment, and every apparent accident is a script.

Slothrop's (P01) first night with Katje (P02): dinner at the glittering Casino, a bawdy drinking song, midnight lovemaking that ends in a pillow-and-Seltzer fight under a red damask tablecloth. Come morning a thief steals every stitch of his clothing; he pursues in a purple bedsheet, crashes among generals playing croquet, and finds his room emptied, Tantivy vanished without trace. The stripping is Pointsman's (P05) programme in action, peeling Slothrop down to naked need so that Katje, carrying Blicero's world inside her, becomes his only refuge. He returns to her door at midnight, soaked and bereft.

Days pass under soft surveillance. Slothrop (P01) studies the V-2 under Sir Stephen Dodson-Truck's tutelage, finding mysterious erections after each lesson; he gets Dodson-Truck catastrophically drunk, and the broken man weeps on the beach, confessing impotence and a lost son. Interleaved is the story of Peter Sachsa and Leni Pökler (P04) in Weimar Berlin, a séance-contact reaching across from the dead, culminating in Sachsa's murder at a street demonstration. Katje whispers that the rocket's parabola and their love are the same shape, and in the morning she is gone, leaving Slothrop exposed once more to Pointsman's (P05) design.

Back at the White Visitation, Pointsman (P05) holds his conspiracy together through budget crises; the PISCES staff meeting is a bureaucratic comic opera. But the episode's true centre is Brigadier Pudding's nocturnal ritual: he creeps through seven antechambers to kneel before Katje (P02) disguised as Domina Nocturna, who canes him, urinates into his mouth, and feeds him her excrement while he weeps in gratitude, the smell recalling Passchendaele's trenches. Pointsman arranged all of it, keeping the old man compliant through shame while Slothrop (P01), far away on the Riviera, remains the absent object of the programme's hunger. Katje's domination of Pudding mirrors her captivity under Blicero: both are theatre, both are real, and both leave her holding someone else's degradation.

Slothrop (P01) is being taught German dialects and propulsion science for deployment into the Zone. Through Hilary Bounce of Shell International, he perceives a sinister corporate web: Shell's liquid-oxygen research, its headquarters used as a German guidance transmitter, Duncan Sandys working from Shell Mex House. He discovers a mysterious parts-list entry for 'Imipolex G', Laszlo Jamf's (P04) aromatic heterocyclic polymer, with a top-secret S-Gerät designation. Reading Tantivy's obituary, gutted by grief, he escapes in a stolen Citroën and reaches Nice under forged papers as 'Ian Scuffling'.

Slothrop (P01), in a borrowed green suit, attends a riotous party where the Hollandaise has been dosed with hashish, a Sherman tank crashes through the garden, and a circular web of black-market debts unravels among forgers and smugglers. Blodgett Waxwing gives Slothrop a zoot suit that once belonged to a Chicano kid beaten in the 1943 Los Angeles Zoot Suit Riots. The provenance of stolen garments runs beneath the slapstick: someone else's suffering is always the fabric you're wearing.

Slothrop (P01) traces Imipolex G through its chemical genealogy: an aromatic heterocyclic polymer invented by Laszlo Jamf (P04) for IG Farben, cross-licensed through Swiss and British firms. In Zurich as 'Ian Scuffling', he haunts espionage cafés, meets the nostalgic Russian Semyavin and the Argentine anarchist Squalidozzi, whose comrades have hijacked a U-boat to seek asylum in ruined Germany. Slothrop camps in Jamf's mountainside crypt awaiting a dossier, sensing the dead chemist's aura around him. The pursuit of the polymer is also a pursuit of origins: who made the substance, who sold it, and what was it designed to touch?

Part 2 ends with institutional collapse. Pointsman (P05), ascendant but cracking, takes his team on a seaside holiday while Slothrop (P01), lost to surveillance for a month, dissolves into the Zone; investigators sent to verify his sexual map find that many of the women simply do not exist. The mythology of the Schwarzkommando (P03) surfaces, real black rocket troops apparently conjured into being by wartime propaganda. Pointsman babbles to himself on the crowded beach while his companions edge away, and the programme dissolves into the noise of its own contradictions.

Slothrop (P01) arrives in the Zone at Nordhausen and reads classified documents linking his family to IG Farben, the Slothrop name threaded through pre-war patent agreements and chemical shipments. He encounters Major Marvy on a train, catches a glimpse of the Schwarzkommando (P03), and is taken in by the young witch Geli Tripping, who tells him about Tchitcherine's (P07) obsessive hunt for Enzian. The Schwarzgerät, the mysterious black device fitted to rocket 00000, becomes the new object of pursuit. Slothrop has crossed from Pointsman's controlled environment into the Zone's chaos, where the documents are thicker but the answers are further away.

Slothrop (P01) descends into the Mittelwerke, the underground V-2 factory tunnels at Nordhausen, wandering among rocket assembly lines in an atmosphere of industrial death. A raucous beer party produces filthy rocket limericks; Major Marvy reappears as pursuer, and Slothrop flees on a miniature rail car with the senile Professor Glimpf. Slapstick chase layered over the tunnels where slave labourers from Dora died by the thousand, the comedy a thin membrane over the horror.

Enzian (P03) and the Schwarzkommando occupy their underground warren near the Mittelwerke, assembling a rocket from scavenged parts while debating their collective fate. The Empty Ones, led by Josef Ombindi, advocate tribal self-extinction as a final refusal of colonial logic; Andreas monitors radio traffic revealing that Enzian and Tchitcherine (P07) share a father. The prose moves between operational tension and deep grief over the Herero genocide, survivors constituted by the annihilation that made them, building a weapon from the technology that was built to destroy everything they were.

Slothrop (P01) and Geli Tripping ascend the Brocken, Faust's mountain, where his own Brockengespenst appears projected in fog, a doubled self he cannot recognise. The Schwarzkommando's (P03) presence shadows the Harz; Enzian's people are consolidating below while Tchitcherine's (P07) intelligence network tightens its search for his half-brother. The tone shifts to antic farce when Slothrop escapes in a hot-air balloon with Schnorp the ex-businessman and they fight a mid-air pie battle against Major Marvy's Henschel plane. The Brocken as Walpurgisnacht: occult dread and vaudeville sharing the same fog.

Tchitcherine's (P07) backstory unfolds: his posting to the Kirghiz steppe to impose a New Turkic Alphabet on oral cultures, his affair with Galina, the drug salesman Wimpe cataloguing opiates like a sommelier, the Baku conference where alphabets are debated as instruments of empire. The episode climaxes with the Kirghiz Light, a numinous desert phenomenon, then excavates the primal scene: his father's liaison with a Herero woman in South-West Africa that produced his half-brother Enzian (P03). Tchitcherine's pursuit of the Schwarzkommando is also a pursuit of the brother he cannot acknowledge, colonial history folded into sibling rivalry.

Slothrop (P01) convalesces in ruined Berlin with the black-marketeer Säure Bummer and reconnects with Seaman Bodine at the Chicago Bar. He acquires the Rocketman costume, cape and helmet, that will become his Zone-wandering persona, a figure lifted from von Göll's (P08) cinematic imagination and now walking the rubble as though the film set were real. The costume is both absurd protection and the beginning of Slothrop's dissolution into myth. Berlin is wreckage, the comedy is exhausted, and the identity Slothrop puts on is already someone else's fiction.

Disguised as 'Max Schlepzig', a name borrowed from one of von Göll's (P08) old films, Slothrop (P01) crosses into the Soviet sector and enters ruined Potsdam, learning en route that Roosevelt has died. He infiltrates the Conference compound, retrieves hidden hashish, glimpses Mickey Rooney performing for troops, and is captured by Tchitcherine's (P07) intelligence unit, who inject him with sodium amytal. Espionage thriller layered over hallucinatory elegy for a dead president and a dead city, where cinema and statecraft share the same stage-set quality.

Argentine anarchists aboard a hijacked U-boat meet the filmmaker von Göll (P08), who proposes shooting the gaucho epic Martín Fierro among the ruins, cinema as revolutionary praxis or confidence trick, nobody sure which. Graciela Imago Portales dreams of Gondwanaland, the primal southern continent before separation. The USS John E. Badass, its crew dosed with Oneirine by Bodine, nearly collides with the submarine in a shared hallucination at sea. Film, narcotics, and continental drift all serve the same logic: reality is negotiable, and the director is whoever holds the camera.

A brief, cold vignette. Tchitcherine (P07) and his driver Dzabajev sit with the stolen hashish, calculating how Slothrop (P01) might be manipulated into leading them to Enzian (P03). Two hunters in a parked vehicle, the Zone's chess game reduced to a cigarette and a waiting engine.

Slothrop (P01) wakes on a film set and meets Margherita Erdmann (P08), a faded UFA actress whose screen-double was played by a man named Max Schlepzig, the very name on Slothrop's forged papers. Their encounter escalates into sadomasochistic sex on a torture-chamber set among props and painted flats, cinema and violence fused into a single act. Tchitcherine's (P07) network still tracks Slothrop's movements through the Zone, but it is the logic of film, not intelligence, that controls this scene. Slothrop is becoming someone else's story.

Franz Pökler's (P04) story spans the full arc of the rocket programme, from Weimar-era amateur rocketry through Peenemünde to the underground factory at Nordhausen. Weissmann, who is Blicero (P02), controls Pökler for years through an annual visit from a girl presented as his daughter Ilse, but who may be a different child each time, a substitution Pökler cannot bear to test. The Kekulé dream links organic chemistry to occult revelation; Fritz Lang's Metropolis, glimpsed in a Berlin cinema, provides the blueprint for the engineer's surrender to the visionary tyrant. Pökler modifies the 00000 rocket's payload to hold a human being without understanding what he is building. At war's end he enters the Dora concentration camp, finds a dying woman, and gives her his wedding ring.

Back in Berlin, Slothrop (P01) and Greta (P08) cohabit uneasily while he brokers hashish through Säure's network and receives a white chess knight from der Springer. Gustav and Säure debate Beethoven (German will-to-power) against Rossini (Italian jouissance, refusal of development) as a kind of aesthetic civil war. A police raid scatters the household; domestic life with Greta sours into mutual exhaustion. The episode's feeling is entropy: Berlin as a city of arguments nobody wins.

Aboard the toiletship Rücksichtslos, the Schwarzkommando (P03) interrogate Achtfaden about the Schwarzgerät and the 00000 rocket's final configuration, the Imipolex G lining that Jamf (P04) designed and Pökler shaped into something capable of holding a human body. Memories of Peenemünde surface: old test launches, the human world behind the technical one. Under pressure Achtfaden gives up the name Klaus Narrisch, another link in the chain toward the device. The investigation is claustrophobic and procedural, Enzian's people following the chemical trail that leads back through the industrial world Jamf and Pökler built.

Slothrop (P01) and Greta (P08) travel by barge toward Swinemünde, stopping at the Bad Karma spa, and board the Anubis, a white yacht named for the jackal-god of death, where an orgy is perpetually underway. Thanatz, Greta's estranged husband, appears and tells Slothrop about the Blicero-Gottfried (P02) pair and the 00000 launch. The child Bianca performs a Shirley Temple routine for the decadent audience: innocence choreographed above an abyss, the body offered as spectacle in a floating theatre of the damned.

Below decks on the Anubis, Slothrop (P01) sleeps with Bianca (P08), a child, in a scene rendered with lyrical explicitness and immediate, devastating withdrawal. She asks him to stay; he refuses, climbing back to the deck, creating what he calls 'a bureaucracy of departure.' The episode opens into an extraordinary direct address: Bianca's coming horror, an elegy linking her to every girl Slothrop has abandoned at roadside lunch counters and Greyhound windows across America. The prose insists the reader is complicit in the leaving.

Ensign Morituri narrates a flashback to Bad Karma in 1939: Margherita (P08), in a psychotic fugue, attacked a child at the mud-bath pools while Morituri struggled to intervene. Back aboard the Anubis, Bianca and Thanatz have disappeared; Greta is hysterical. Morituri mentions his desire to return to Hiroshima, a single word that detonates forward through the rest of the book. Slothrop (P01) drifts through the aftermath, and the Anubis sequence closes with loss confirmed but never shown: Bianca is simply gone, another child consumed by the world Blicero (P02) set in motion.

Margherita Erdmann (P08) tells Slothrop (P01) the story of her life: her multiple film identities, her scar-covered body from Thanatz's whippings, and her final days with Blicero (P02). She was driven to a petrochemical plant where industrialists held a séance-like board meeting over a grey plastic object meant for the S-Gerät; men dressed her in arousing Imipolex G garments in curtained plastic chambers. She submitted, was abandoned naked, walked back to find the battery deserted, Blicero and Gottfried gone. A confession, intimate and lurid, delivered from the Anubis, where the film actress's body and the rocket's plastic skin turn out to be the same material.

The Anubis drives through a Baltic storm toward Swinemünde, St. Elmo's fire flickering in the rigging. Slothrop (P01) registers growing emotional numbness; searching for Bianca (P08), he sees, or thinks he sees, her slip overboard in the storm, and lunges after her into the freezing Oder Haff. The narration drifts into prophetic future tense, dissolving certainty about what has happened and what will happen. The Anubis sails on without him, its theatre continuing in his absence.

Slothrop (P01) is fished from the water by Frau Gnahb, a manic black-market smuggler, and her son Otto. At Swinemünde he finds der Springer, who turns out to be the filmmaker von Göll (P08), and they set off up the Baltic coast toward Peenemünde with a cargo of chorus girls, performing chimpanzees, and vodka. At Peenemünde the Russians seize Springer, and the troupe mounts a chaotic rescue: fake Molotov cocktails, dancing girls as decoys, a confrontation with Tchitcherine (P07) underground. A commando raid staged as vaudeville, with Slothrop at its centre wearing someone else's identity.

At Peenemünde's test stands, the narrative pauses to enunciate 'Mondaugen's Law': personal density is proportional to temporal bandwidth. Slothrop (P01) is beginning to scatter, to lose the thread of his own quest; they reach the Holy Centre of the rocket's ellipse and feel nothing. Inside the tunnel complex they find Springer (P08) drugged, trick Tchitcherine's (P07) guards, and flee. Narrisch, left behind with a tommy gun, waits in a drainpipe, his death framed in the language of rocket ballistics.

Three brief movements. Enzian (P03), Andreas, and Christian of the Schwarzkommando burst into a room where Ombindi's Empty Ones have forced an abortion on Christian's sister Maria, then race through bombed Hamburg on motorcycles to find her husband Pavel, lost in gasoline hallucinations at the ruined Jamf Ölfabriken refinery. Christian nearly shoots Pavel through a rifle sight before Enzian knocks the barrel aside; separately, Slothrop (P01) wakes in Swinemünde and Springer (P08) offers him a forged discharge in exchange for one more piracy. The Schwarzkommando's struggle to preserve life against the Empty Ones' nihilism runs counter to the sacrifice that Blicero has already ordained.

Springer's boat chases the Anubis along the coast of Rügen in rain and rough seas. Frau Gnahb rams the yacht; Slothrop (P01) boards and descends into pitch darkness to retrieve a package from the engine room, where someone beats him and forces him down a ladder to encounter something hanging: a body in cold silk, dead flesh smelling of the sea. The episode leaps to London, where Katje leaves the White Visitation and Pirate Prentice (P06) begins organising the Counterforce. Meanwhile the Schwarzkommando debate between Enzian's (P03) will to continue and Ombindi's doctrine of the Final Zero, tribal self-extinction set against colonial survival. The threads are pulling apart: Slothrop descending, the Counterforce forming, the Herero dividing.

An allegorical set-piece: Pirate Prentice (P06) arrives at an enormous gathering of double agents, informers, and the morally compromised, following a ball of taffy through labyrinthine corridors. Father Rapier preaches that 'They' may have achieved immortality and harvest our deaths. Pirate meets his group: Sir Stephen Dodson-Truck, the Welsh informer Merciful Evans, and Katje (P02) herself. They confront shared guilt, bargain over loyalty, and dance together, a community of the damned finding no redemption but a fragile solidarity.

Slothrop (P01) wakes in a burned-out locksmith's shop in Stralsund, alone, and drifts southward through the great summer migration of displaced persons: Poles, Czechs, Gypsies, POWs, Wehrmacht veterans. He sleeps in abandoned farmhouses and dreams of dead Tantivy, who tells him 'I'm not looking after you.' Roger (P06) and Pirate and the nascent Counterforce are elsewhere, organising in London, and nobody comes to find the man they were meant to save. Slothrop encounters Ludwig, a fat boy chasing his escaped lemming Ursula northward toward the Baltic; the pace is slow, pastoral, and saturated with loss.

Slothrop (P01) and Ludwig arrive at a coastal town where, in a church basement, Major Marvy and Bloody Chiclitz run a black-market fur operation staffed by children. Posing as a Russian officer in Tchitcherine's (P07) stolen uniform, Slothrop learns that a raid on the Schwarzkommando (P03) is planned for midnight and walks across meadows at dusk to warn Andreas and the Zone-Hereros. They receive him calmly, feed him, and explain their rocket mandala, KEZVH: a sacred diagram combining male and female, birth and fire, the ancestors at the centre. For one evening Slothrop belongs somewhere, admitted to a meaning he has been chasing without knowing its shape.

Aftermath of the failed raid. Tchitcherine (P07) sits in a charred clearing with Marvy and Chiclitz, drunk and arguing; he perceives a 'Rocket-cartel' spanning nations, IG Farben, Siemens, Krupp, General Electric, a state beyond states with the Rocket as its soul. A hallucinatory giant Finger appears, pointing him toward this meta-conspiracy, while Marvy rages about exterminating the Schwarzkommando (P03). Slothrop has drifted on, and Enzian's people survive another night, but Tchitcherine realises he is on the outside of something enormous.

Slothrop (P01) drifts toward Cuxhaven through north German countryside, conscripted by local children to play Plechazunga, the Pig-Hero, in their annual festival. At the ghost-town Zwölfkinder he meets Franz Pökler (P04), who tells him about Imipolex G and its role in the Schwarzgerät. The Schwarzkommando (P03) under Enzian are tracing the same trail through different channels, and Tchitcherine's intelligence network closes from the other direction; all three hunts now converge on the same object. Pökler's lost daughter Ilse merges in Slothrop's mind with the dead Bianca, another child the machine has consumed.

Pökler (P04) tells Slothrop (P01) about Laszlo Jamf's final lectures: his hatred of the covalent bond, his worship of the ionic, his exhortations toward inorganic synthesis. The episode is a history of Pökler's intellectual seduction, traced through Fritz Lang's films where Klein-Rogge played Mabuse and Attila and the mad inventor Rothwang, each role a template for charismatic destruction. Jamf emerges as prophet of death-in-synthesis, though he himself never moved beyond carbon chemistry. Slothrop listens, absorbing the genealogy of the substance his body was conditioned to desire.

The biography of Lyle Bland, American plutocrat: his manipulation of patents, his suppression of fuel-efficient carburettors, his interlocking interests with IG Farben and GE's Swope. Then Bland's accidental initiation into real Masonic magic; he begins leaving his body, perceives Earth as a living creature, and one evening announces to his family that he is departing forever. The Metropolis passage names the Corporate City-state where the engineer serves the administrator and cinema provides the blueprint. A pinball-machine episode in Mouthorgan, Missouri, connects Slothrop's (P01) family to the cartel network through which Pökler's (P04) work was financed and Jamf's research was weaponised. Pökler's complicity is sealed; he will not appear again.

In Cuxhaven, Slothrop (P01) drifts through the Operation Backfire zone in his pig suit, scattering further. Two surgeons, Muffage and Spontoon, have been dispatched by Pointsman (P05) to castrate 'Slothrop', but Slothrop encounters Seaman Bodine at a runcible-spoon fight, hijacks a Red Cross Clubmobile with a girl named Shirley, and races toward Putzi's, a sprawling manor of bars, brothels, and opium dens. At Putzi's the promised discharge never arrives, and Slothrop collapses in paranoid despair in a coat closet.

Major Marvy stumbles into Slothrop's (P01) abandoned pig suit at Putzi's during an MP raid and is seized by the surgeons, who mistake him for their target. Strapped to a stretcher and sedated, Marvy is driven to a moonlit beach where Muffage and Spontoon perform a swift orchidectomy, castrating the wrong man. Meanwhile Slothrop sleeps peacefully with Solange, who is in fact Leni Pökler (P04), dreaming of her daughter Ilse riding lost freight trains through the Zone. Blackly comic surgical slapstick with terrible consequences, and Slothrop slipping further from the identity everyone is trying to seize.

Tchitcherine (P07) watches from a juniper copse as two guitarists perform for a circle of townspeople, and Squalidozzi's Argentine anarchists have built a real community on a von Göll (P08) film set; Felipe worships sentient rocks. Elsewhere the Zone holds stranger villages: a Palestinian commune, a dog-city ruled by abandoned Dobermans. In London, Pointsman (P05) is disgraced after the Marvy castration; Sir Marcus and Clive Mossmoon discuss operations against the Schwarzkommando in tones of camp sadism. Slothrop's name no longer appears in the dispatches, and Tchitcherine, like everyone else, is searching for a man who is ceasing to exist.

Pirate Prentice (P06) flies a green P-47 toward Berlin, singing, haunted by the ghost of Katje's ancestor Frans van der Groov. Slothrop (P01) has retreated to mountain solitude in the Harz, growing wild, playing harmonica by a stream, reading omens in bird flights and trout guts; 'ROCKETMAN WAS HERE' appears on a shithouse wall as his last legible signature. Gustav and Säure argue about Beethoven against Rossini, tonality against the twelve-tone row, while von Göll's (P08) cinematic fictions continue to circulate through the Zone like currency. The Counterforce assembles, but the man they were meant to rescue is already dissolving.

Roger Mexico (P06), having lost Jessica to peacetime and to Jeremy, drives a stolen Horch across the Lüneburg Heath singing heartbroken love songs. He learns from Milton Gloaming that IG Farben had Slothrop (P01) under special surveillance before the war, the corporate web reaching further back than anyone knew. Roger storms into Twelfth House, terrorises a German secretary, invades Clive Mossmoon's office, and urinates on the assembled power-brokers, threatening Pointsman (P05) with permanent haunting. He escapes to Pirate's maisonette and joins the Counterforce, but the gesture of defiance is already tinged with futility.

American soldiers sweep Thuringia for hidden weapons. Pfc. Eddie Pensiero gives a colonel a painstaking haircut while the colonel dreams of Laszlo Jamf (P04) and a guide called 'Mister Information' who delivers a fable about the War as a system that kills those who cannot be used. Then the extraordinary embedded tale of Byron the Bulb, an immortal lightbulb born in Weimar Berlin, hunted by the Phoebus cartel, passing from opium dens to Nazi rallies, gathering knowledge of all power systems yet remaining forever impotent to change them. It is the Counterforce's (P06) dilemma made parable: to see everything and alter nothing.

Katje Borgesius (P02) arrives by bicycle at the Schwarzkommando settlement and meets Enzian (P03); both were loved by Blicero, and their conversation is a careful, wounding negotiation about shared grief. Enzian tells Katje her story is the saddest because she has been 'set free', condemned to survive without purpose. She wants to understand what she did to Slothrop (P01) and whether 'They' might be stopped, carrying the Counterforce's question into the Herero camp. Enzian offers to let her hear testimony about Blicero's last days, and for a moment two of the novel's loneliest figures sit together in the knowledge that the man who ruined them both is still directing events from the past.

Thanatz, washed overboard from the Anubis, is rescued by a lightning-obsessed Polish undertaker and stumbles into a community of '175s', homosexual concentration camp survivors who have reconstructed a phantom SS hierarchy with Blicero (P02) as their supreme commander. Weeks of freight trains, beatings, and fever strip Thanatz bare. His memory of the 00000 firing clarifies: Blicero's eyes reflecting a windmill that was not there, visions of Gottfried and Katje merging into a single figure of sacrifice. The Schwarzkommando (P03) retrieve him, and he speaks for seven hours, revealing everything about the Schwarzgerät, the Imipolex G shroud, and the last launch. Thanatz's testimony is the novel's long-delayed witness account, the moment the 00000's horror becomes speakable.

A vast, polyphonic episode held together by the Raketen-Stadt. Slothrop (P01) appears as a comic-book kid whose own father tries to kill him daily; his rescue team is a broken Grail fellowship. Interpolated scenes multiply: a letter from Slothrop's mother to Ambassador Kennedy, Säure burgling an astrologer, the properties of Imipolex G, Slothrop in transvestite disguise handed an anarchist bomb by an orangutan, kamikaze pilots Takeshi and Ichizo on their forgotten island, a meditation on Hiroshima. The Counterforce's (P06) ambitions scatter alongside Slothrop himself; vaudeville variety show unravelling into terror.

Tchitcherine (P07), abandoned by Dzabajev, drifts through the Zone remembering Wimpe the IG Farben salesman's philosophy of narcotics as instruments of transcendence. An extended section on Oneirine describes its 'haunting' effect: recurring visions violated by impossibility. A figure like Ripov interrogates Tchitcherine, coaxing damning confessions about why he hunted his half-brother Enzian (P03); the agents tell him the Schwarzkommando are being taken from him. He is left alone among melted plastic toothbrushes, his pursuit collapsing into hallucination.

The Counterforce (P06) in action, and in failure. At the Gross Suckling Conference, Roger Mexico works out the rocket mandala's missing bearing; he confronts Jeremy over Jessica, then arrives at a Krupp executive's dinner with Seaman Bodine in an outrageous zoot suit. Together they enact the Disgusting Dinner: snot soup, pus pudding, smegma stew, sending the industrialists vomiting from the table while a Haydn 'Kazoo Quartet' plays in the background. Jessica weeps and is led away by Jeremy forever; the Counterforce's inevitable co-optation makes the disgust ring hollow.

Geli Tripping (P07) sets out across the Zone to find Tchitcherine, carrying his toenail clippings and a love-charm, feeling Pan and the Titans stirring at the edges of the Harz mountains. Intercut is Blicero's (P02) devastating monologue to Gottfried about America as Death's gift to Europe, about wanting to be 'taken in love' beyond the cycle of infection, while Gottfried kneels in his dog collar, sensing he is being asked to decide something. The Counterforce gropes toward the same truth from the political end, but it is Geli's magic and Blicero's sacrificial theology that hold the real charge. The scene is left suspended, like the Fool card.

Enzian (P03) leads the Schwarzkommando and their assembled 00001 rocket northward in a tense night-trek: disguises, tarpaulins over rocket sections, a fake Frank Sinatra, a hostile ambush killing Orutyene. At a deserted sonic research station, Katje (P02), Enzian, and Christian shelter together. Enzian confronts Ombindi of the Empty Ones over their competing visions of the Herero future, tribal suicide against continuation, while the 00001 they carry mirrors the 00000 that Blicero fired with Gottfried inside it. The column drives toward the firing site on the Lüneburg Heath, carrying both an answer and a question.

Geli Tripping's (P07) love-spell works: she finds Tchitcherine by a stream in a culvert, and they become lovers. At nightfall a Schwarzkommando convoy passes over the bridge above them; Tchitcherine climbs up and encounters Enzian (P03) face to face, his half-brother, but neither recognises the other. They exchange cigarettes and a polite nod, and Enzian rides on toward the launch that will answer Blicero's (P02) sacrifice with its own. The narrator observes: this is magic, but not the first time a man has passed his brother without knowing it.

The novel's coda. Slothrop (P01) has scattered entirely; only a photograph, a bloodstained undershirt, a bottle of May wine remain, and Bodine cannot hold him together. Enzian (P03) and the Schwarzkommando assemble their 00001 at the Lüneburg site; Weissmann's Tarot is read. Then the 00000 firing in full ritual: Gottfried wrapped in Imipolex, Blicero (P02) speaking the launch sequence in German, the flame turning blue, Gottfried's consciousness thinning toward white. We end in a Los Angeles movie theatre where Richard Zhlubb rants about harmonicas, the screen goes dark, and a rocket falls toward the roof; a hymn by William Slothrop closes the book at the last delta-t, inviting the audience to sing.
